questionText: When a class shows uneven learning progress, what is your first response as a school leader?
opt0_text: Ask teachers to continue as planned so most students stay on track
opt1_text: Ask teachers to identify specific students who need different support
opt2_text: Review overall class performance trends
opt3_text: Discuss strategies collectively in the staff meeting

questionText: When you visit a classroom, what makes you feel that learning is happening well?
opt0_text: Students are attentive and engaged in activities
opt1_text: The teacher is explaining concepts clearly
opt2_text: Students are asking questions and trying different approaches
opt3_text: Students are able to answer assessment questions correctly

questionText: When a teacher’s lesson does not go as expected, what do you usually do first?
opt0_text: Ask the teacher to reflect on what worked and what did not
opt1_text: Suggest using a different activity next time
opt2_text: Check student assessment results
opt3_text: Discuss the issue with other teachers

questionText: When reviewing student assessment data, what do you focus on first?
opt0_text: Overall class average
opt1_text: Common misconceptions across students
opt2_text: Individual students who are struggling
opt3_text: Sharing findings with teachers for planning

questionText: When planning improvement efforts in the school, who do you involve early?
opt0_text: Senior teachers
opt1_text: Parents and caregivers
opt2_text: Focus on internal planning first
opt3_text: Use assessment data to guide decisions

questionText: When reviewing classroom observations, which pattern concerns you the most?
opt0_text: Students finishing work at different times
opt1_text: Students asking many questions during the lesson
opt2_text: Students working quietly but showing little initiative
opt3_text: Students discussing answers with peers

questionText: How do you usually understand students’ varied learning preferences?
opt0_text: Through professional conversation with teachers
opt1_text: From student classroom behaviour
opt2_text: From assessment results
opt3_text: From parent feedback about learning at home

questionText: When walking into a classroom, what do you naturally look at first?
opt0_text: Whether students are attentive
opt1_text: Whether the teacher is following the plan
opt2_text: Whether different students are engaged in different ways
opt3_text: Whether the class is quiet and orderly

questionText: If multiple classes struggle with a topic, what is your typical next step?
opt0_text: Check whether the chapters were completed
opt1_text: Compare performance data across classes
opt2_text: Discuss with teachers how students approached the topic
opt3_text: Ask teachers to schedule additional revision of the same content

questionText: When looking at learning data, which trend do you investigate first?
opt0_text: Number of students who scored below 40%
opt1_text: Variation between question types
opt2_text: Comparisons between sections of the same grade
opt3_text: Shifts in performance of specific students over time

questionText: During a class observation, if students complete a task very quickly, you generally think:
opt0_text: The task was easy and appropriate
opt1_text: The teacher’s explanation was very clear
opt2_text: The task may not have challenged all learners
opt3_text: Students must have memorised similar questions

questionText: When reviewing lesson plans, which aspect do you notice most?
opt0_text: Whether the content matches the textbook
opt1_text: Whether the activities are manageable within the period
opt2_text: Whether the intended outcomes are measurable
opt3_text: Whether instructions are clear for the teacher to follow

questionText: A teacher asks for support in improving student understanding. Your first question is usually:
opt0_text: Which part of the syllabus is pending?
opt1_text: What tasks did students struggle with?
opt2_text: How did students respond during the lesson?
opt3_text: Which worksheets have been completed so far?

questionText: When teachers prepare activities, what is your main consideration?
opt0_text: Whether the activity is manageable for all classes within time
opt1_text: Whether the activity matches the exam format
opt2_text: Whether students must think in different ways during the activity
opt3_text: Whether the activity is co-planned or shareable

questionText: When you see a teacher using an unfamiliar method, what do you usually do?
opt0_text: Check whether class control is maintained
opt1_text: Observe student reactions closely
opt2_text: Ask the teacher to explain the thinking behind it later
opt3_text: Remind them to stay aligned with the yearly plan

questionText: When a teacher struggles repeatedly with a concept, your instinctive response is:
opt0_text: Ask another teacher to share materials
opt1_text: Suggest attending an upcoming workshop
opt2_text: Sit together to understand the root difficulty
opt3_text: Recommend revising the chapter again

questionText: During staff meetings, what do you tend to highlight the most?
opt0_text: Completion of planned activities
opt1_text: Instances of effective teamwork
opt2_text: Student progress trends
opt3_text: Challenges teachers are facing

questionText: When planning parent interactions, what is your priority?
opt0_text: Ensuring logistical arrangements are smooth
opt1_text: Updating parents about academic schedules
opt2_text: Helping parents understand how learning happens
opt3_text: Addressing specific concerns raised by parents

questionText: When parents ask about their child’s progress, you usually talk about:
opt0_text: Attendance and day-to-day behaviour
opt1_text: Upcoming school activities
opt2_text: How the child learns and participates
opt3_text: Marks and test performance

questionText: When designing the school communication plan, you focus on:
opt0_text: Frequency of communication
opt1_text: Ensuring messages are clear and short
opt2_text: Helping parents support learning at home
opt3_text: Making sure all notices reach parents
